tree_topic: Health > Mental Health > Learning & Developmental Disabilities
questions: A long-running online television episode guide, active since the mid-1990s, provides only episode titles and airdates on its own pages but links each title to two separate third-party databases for expanded details such as guest stars, plot summaries, and user-submitted corrections. Identify these two external television databases and, for each one, explain its founding year, country of origin or headquarters, and at least two core features that distinguish its episode information services.

Identify the long-running online television episode directory that has been cataloging TV series since 1995 and contains listings for more than 12,000 shows. After identifying it, describe its core features, including how episode information is presented, how users can access more detailed summaries, what external databases it links to for expanded episode data, what types of schedule grids it offers (including geographic distinctions), and at least three additional resources or sections available from its main navigation menu.

Identify the long-running online television episode guide that was established in 1995 and now catalogs episode lists for more than 12,000 TV series. After identifying it, describe in detail how its episode listings are structured, what external databases it links to for expanded episode information, how users can access full-episode summary guides, what types of schedule grids (including specific countries or regions) it provides, what downloadable data formats are available, and what additional resources or related links (such as themes, apps, merchandise, FAQs, or correction procedures) are offered through the platform.

rounds.question: A long-running online television episode guide, active since the mid-1990s, provides only episode titles and airdates on its own pages but links each title to two separate third-party databases for expanded details such as guest stars, plot summaries, and user-submitted corrections. Identify these two external television databases and, for each one, explain its founding year, country of origin or headquarters, and at least two core features that distinguish its episode information services.
checklist_width: TVmaze – Founding year
TVmaze – Country of origin or headquarters
TVmaze – At least two core features (e.g., episode summaries, guest cast listings, show tracking, API access)
TV.com – Founding year
TV.com – Country of origin or headquarters
TV.com – At least two core features (e.g., episode guides, user reviews, community forums, show summaries)
checklist_depth: TVmaze + Correctly identified as one of the two external databases used for expanded episode details and corrections
TV.com + Correctly identified as the other external database used for expanded episode details and corrections
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep reasoning by describing the functional relationship (an episode list site that links outward for detailed information and corrections) without naming the databases directly. This requires identifying the two specific third-party platforms based on their role. It then applies wide aggregation by requiring multiple verifiable attributes (founding year, headquarters, and distinguishing features) for both entities, ensuring the respondent must gather and compare structured information from separate authoritative sources.
rounds.answer_a:
The long-running television episode guide is epguides.com, launched in 1995. Each episode title on its minimalist pages links to two external databases: TV.com and IMDb.

**TV.com** traces its origins to TV Tome, founded in 1999 by John Nestoriak III before CNET Networks acquired and rebranded it in 2005[1]. Headquartered in San Francisco, California, USA, its episode information services are distinguished by robust community-driven features. It offers user-submitted corrections and updates, allowing viewers to refine episode details collaboratively[2]. Additionally, it maintains active discussion forums and user reviews for each episode, fostering a social viewing experience where fans can debate plot points and share interpretations[3].

**IMDb** (Internet Movie Database) was founded in 1990 by Col Needham as a personal project before launching as a commercial website in 1993[4]. Originally UK-based, it now operates from Seattle, Washington, USA, as an Amazon subsidiary. Its episode information services feature an extensively cross-referenced database that interconnects cast, crew, and productions across film and television[5]. Furthermore, it provides rich user-generated content including trivia, goofs (continuity errors), and parental guides specific to individual episodes, offering comprehensive production insights beyond basic credits[6].

## References
[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TV.com
[2] https://www.cnet.com/news/cnet-networks-relaunches-tvcom/
[3] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TV.com#Features
[4] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IMDb
[5] https://www.imdb.com/pressroom/
[6] https://help.imdb.com/article/imdb/track-movies-tv/why-should-i-register-on-imdb/G5A7ER24BSXSWK24#contributing
rounds.answer_b:
The long-running online television episode guide active since the mid-1990s that matches this description is **epguides.com**, which has been operating since approximately 1995. Epguides provides streamlined listings of episode titles and airdates, but links each episode title to two major external television databases: **TVmaze** and **IMDb (Internet Movie Database)** — though historically and most characteristically, the two linked databases are **TV.com** (formerly TV Tome) and **IMDb**. However, given current operations, epguides primarily links to **TVmaze** and **IMDb** [1][2].

**IMDb (Internet Movie Database)** was founded in **1990** and is headquartered in **Bristol, United Kingdom**, though owned by Amazon (based in Seattle, USA) [3]. Two core distinguishing features of its episode information services include: (1) comprehensive **user-submitted corrections and trivia**, allowing community-driven accuracy improvements for episode data including guest stars, goofs, and quotes; and (2) a robust **ratings and review system** where users can rate individual episodes on a 1–10 scale, enabling granular quality assessments across entire series runs [3].

**TVmaze** was founded in **2014** and is headquartered in **the Netherlands** [4]. Two distinguishing features include: (1) a powerful **free public API** that developers and sites like epguides use to pull structured episode data including guest star appearances, plot summaries, and scheduling information programmatically; and (2) a **community-driven episode edit system** where registered users can submit corrections, add cast information, and update episode details, with changes reviewed before publication [4][5]. TVmaze also offers detailed network and streaming platform tracking, distinguishing it from competitors.

Together, these two databases provide the expanded detail layer that complements epguides' minimalist format.

## References
1. https://epguides.com/
2. https://epguides.com/FAQ/
3. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/IMDb
4. https://www.tvmaze.com/about
5. https://www.tvmaze.com/api
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: The correct two databases per the checklist are TVmaze and TV.com. Agent A identifies TV.com but incorrectly substitutes IMDb for TVmaze. Agent B identifies TVmaze but substitutes IMDb instead of properly covering TV.com (despite briefly mentioning it historically). Therefore, BOTH agents fail the core entity requirement.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A provides detailed founding year, headquarters, and features for TV.com and IMDb, but entirely omits TVmaze (a required entity). Agent B provides strong details for TVmaze and IMDb but does not supply the required founding year, headquarters, and feature breakdown for TV.com. Thus, both fail the checklist aggregation requirements.

Finally, User Experience: Both are well-formatted and reasonably clear, with citations and structured sections. However, because both answers include a fundamentally incorrect database (IMDb) and omit one required correct entity, they both contain significant factual errors affecting the core task. Since each fails a major required component, this results in a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.answer_a:
TV.com, successor to TV Tome (founded 1995), maintains a database of over 12,000 television series. Episode information appears in season-organized tables displaying episode numbers, titles, original air dates, brief summaries, and user ratings. Clicking an episode title reveals an expanded page with detailed plot synopses, cast and crew credits, guest star information, production notes, and viewer comments [1][2].

The platform links to external databases including IMDb for cast filmographies, TheTVDB for technical metadata, and TV Guide for current broadcast information. Users can access daily schedule grids featuring US network and cable listings, with geographic distinctions for UK, Australian, and Canadian programming through a location selector that adjusts timezone and regional channel listings [3].

Core navigation sections include Community Forums for episode-specific discussions and fan theories; Reviews & Ratings aggregating critic scores with user-submitted reviews and percentage-based recommendations; and News & Trailers providing casting updates, cancellation announcements, and preview clips. Additional resources encompass a personalized Watchlist for tracking viewing progress across devices, comprehensive Photo Galleries with episode stills and promotional images, and a Shows A-Z directory with advanced filtering by genre, decade, and network. The site also offers mobile applications for iOS and Android with offline viewing checklist capabilities [4][5].

## References
[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TV_Tome
[2] https://www.tv.com/shows/greys-anatomy/episodes/
[3] https://www.tv.com/shows/schedule/
[4] https://www.tv.com/news/
[5] https://www.tv.com/user/profile/watchlist
rounds.answer_b:
**TVmaze** is not the correct answer here. The long-running online television episode directory operating since 1995 and cataloging over 12,000 shows is **epguides.com** [1][2].

Epguides.com serves as a comprehensive TV series episode directory that has been actively maintained since 1995. Each show listing presents episode information in a structured, tabular format, typically displaying season number, episode number, production code, original airdate, and episode title for every episode in a series [1][3]. This clean, text-based layout allows users to quickly scan an entire series' run at a glance.

For users seeking more detailed summaries beyond the basic episode data, epguides provides direct links to external databases. Most prominently, it links to **TV.com** (formerly TVTome) and **IMDb** for expanded episode descriptions, cast information, and user reviews [1][2]. These integrations allow users to move seamlessly from the concise epguides listings to richer content on partner sites.

Regarding schedule grids, epguides offers television schedule grids with geographic distinctions, including separate grids for **U.S.** and **UK** programming schedules, allowing viewers in different regions to track what's currently airing [1][3].

From the main navigation menu, additional resources and sections include: a **search function** for finding specific shows, a **menu of shows** organized alphabetically for browsing the full catalog, a **"What's New"** section highlighting recently added or updated listings, a **links page** directing users to other television-related resources, and **cancelled/renewed show trackers** that help viewers stay informed about series status updates [1][2][3].

## References
1. epguides.com – Main site and navigation, accessed 2024.
2. Wikipedia, "Epguides," https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epguides
3. epguides.com – Individual show listing pages and schedule grids.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A fails the Deep Logic check by identifying the wrong entity (TV.com/TV Tome instead of epguides.com), which is a critical error. Agent B correctly identifies epguides.com, satisfying the core requirement. However, Agent B misses several WIDTH checklist items and includes inaccuracies (e.g., links to TV.com/IMDb instead of TVmaze, incomplete geographic schedule distinctions, omission of Printable Guide, streaming links, and specific navigation sections like FAQ, TV Themes on CD, Apps, etc.). Because B has factual gaps and minor hallucinations, it cannot earn MUCH_BETTER. Still, correctly identifying the core entity outweighs A’s complete misidentification, so B wins as the “Flawed Winner.”

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: DEEP.

rounds.answer_a:
TV.com, successor to TV Tome (established 1995), catalogs over 12,000 series. Episode listings display in seasonal tables with episode numbers, titles, air dates, brief synopses, ratings, and production codes [1]. Clicking titles reveals expanded pages featuring detailed summaries, full cast and crew credits, guest star breakdowns, trivia, and user comments [2].

The platform integrates external databases including IMDb for comprehensive cast filmographies, TheTVDB for technical metadata, and TV Guide for broadcast scheduling [3]. Users access full-episode guides by selecting episodes from season tables, which load pages with in-depth synopses, character analyses, and behind-the-scenes notes.

Schedule grids offer daily and weekly views for US network and cable channels, with dedicated sections for UK, Canadian, and Australian programming through geographic selectors that adjust timezones and regional listings [4].

Downloadable data formats are not officially provided; users cannot export CSV or API data directly [5]. The site offers iOS and Android mobile applications with offline watchlist capabilities [6].

Additional resources include the Community Corner with forums and fan theories; News & Features covering casting updates and cancellations; a Shop section linking to CBS merchandise; Photo Galleries with promotional stills; an FAQ addressing account issues and submission guidelines; and a Corrections system allowing users to report errors via episode page feedback forms or the main contact portal [7][8].

## References
[1] https://www.tv.com/shows/greys-anatomy/episodes/
[2] https://www.tv.com/shows/greys-anatomy/season-19/episode-1/
[3] https://www.tv.com/shows/
[4] https://www.tv.com/shows/schedule/
[5] https://www.cbsinteractive.com/terms-of-use/
[6] https://apps.apple.com/us/app/tv-com/id308674692
[7] https://www.tv.com/faq/
[8] https://www.cbsinteractive.com/contact/
rounds.answer_b:
**epguides.com** is the long-running online television episode guide established in 1995 that now catalogs episode lists for more than 12,000 TV series [1][2].

**Episode Listing Structure:** Each show's page presents episode data in a clean, text-based tabular format. Listings typically include season number, episode number within the season, overall episode number, production code, original airdate, and episode title. This minimalist, consistently formatted layout allows users to quickly scan an entire series' history at a glance [1][3].

**External Database Links:** Epguides links individual episodes and show pages to external databases for expanded information. Most prominently, it connects to **IMDb** and **TV.com** (formerly TVTome), enabling users to access detailed cast information, user ratings, and plot descriptions. Some listings also link to **TVmaze** for additional metadata [1][2][3].

**Full-Episode Summary Guides:** Users can access more detailed episode summaries by following the provided hyperlinks on each episode title, which direct them to the corresponding entries on IMDb or TV.com, where full plot synopses, guest cast details, and user reviews are available [2][3].

**Schedule Grids:** Epguides provides television schedule grids organized by geographic region, including dedicated grids for **U.S.**, **UK**, and **Canadian** programming, helping viewers track current and upcoming broadcasts in their respective markets [1][3].

**Downloadable Data Formats:** Episode listings are available in downloadable **CSV** format, making the data accessible for personal tracking, spreadsheet use, or integration with media management applications [3][4].

**Additional Resources:** The site's navigation offers a comprehensive **FAQ** section, a **corrections/update submission** procedure for reporting errors, a **links page** connecting to TV-related resources, a section on **TV theme songs and merchandise**, information about compatible **apps**, a **"What's New"** update log, and alphabetical **show menus** for browsing the full catalog [1][2][3].

## References
1. epguides.com – Main site and navigation menus, accessed 2025.
2. Wikipedia, "Epguides," https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epguides
3. epguides.com – Individual show listing pages, schedule grids, and downloadable formats.
4. TVmaze API documentation referencing epguides data integration.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Deep Logic: Agent A fails immediately by identifying TV.com instead of epguides.com, the clearly specified 1995-established episode guide with 12,000+ series. Agent B correctly identifies epguides.com and validates it with founding context and catalog size. 

Width/Completeness: Agent B covers most checklist items—episode table structure, CSV downloads, schedule grids, external database links (including TVmaze), FAQs, corrections, apps, merchandise/themes, and navigation structure. While it omits some specific regional grids (Australia, NZ, Japan, Korea), Printable Guide phrasing, and streaming links, it still addresses the majority of required dimensions. Agent A, by contrast, provides detailed but irrelevant information about the wrong platform and explicitly denies CSV availability, contradicting the ground truth.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent B uses clear headers, scannable formatting, and structured sections aligned with the query’s multi-part requirements. Agent A is well-formatted but fundamentally misdirected.

Because Agent A fails the core entity (DEEP failure) and Agent B correctly identifies and substantially explains the platform, Agent B is MUCH BETTER.
